CAR & OJ: Perfect together

Ralph Frammolino Los Angeles Times December 1994
Data & Databases

Ralph Frammolino discusses the application of computer-assisted reporting (CAR) in creating a detailed jury profile for the O.J. Simpson trial, highlighting the effectiveness of newly acquired skills.

The Los Angeles Times recently invited NICAR to town to put me and nine other reporters, editors and researchers through a week-long computer-assisted journalism boot camp. The goal was to lighten the load on the paper’s overworked in-house computer gurus by introducing at least a few newsroom types to the rudiments of transferring files and analyzing statistics on a spreadsheet or database program.

Our mission was to overcome the fear of ASCII, DOS, ANSI, and a handful of other tortured acronyms, and to eventually integrate the powerful tool of computer analysis into daily reporting or projects. But no sooner had I walked back into the newsroom, my newly acquired ‘expertise’ was put to the test on the O.J. Simpson murder trial. Facing a tight deadline, we were to profile the jury pool using a three-page form of responses filled by the prospective jurors.

Instead of a simple tally, NICAR training enabled us to do a more extensive analysis using Microsoft Access. By looking at the questionnaires, we recorded details such as age, race, and media exposure. Initially overwhelmed, we dug into the database, putting together a profile that highlighted significant insights. Most notably, we found statistical differences in perceptions about DNA evidence among jurors still in the pool versus those excused.
